text.title: Kubernetes Production Configuration
text.summary: Resource requests/limits, health probes, security context hardening, PodDisruptionBudget, and image policy.

rule.statement: Always set resource requests AND limits on every container; base requests on P95 observed usage, not guesses.
rule.rationale: Without requests, the scheduler cannot make correct node placement decisions. Without limits, a runaway process can consume all node memory and evict neighboring pods. Requests set to P95 usage prevent 99.94% over-provisioning seen in typical clusters.

example.code: resources: requests: memory: "256Mi" cpu: "250m" limits: memory: "512Mi" cpu: "500m" # consider omitting CPU limit to avoid throttling
example.note: CPU limits cause throttling even when node capacity is available. Many teams omit CPU limits and rely on requests for scheduling. Memory limits remain critical — OOMKill is a hard boundary.

antipattern.description: Setting requests equal to limits (Guaranteed QoS) for all pods.
antipattern.reason: Guaranteed QoS is appropriate for latency-sensitive workloads. For most workloads, Burstable QoS (requests lower than limits) allows better bin-packing and handles traffic spikes without OOMKill.

rule.statement: Configure all three probes: startupProbe for slow-starting apps, readinessProbe to gate traffic, livenessProbe to restart deadlocked containers.
rule.rationale: Without startupProbe, livenessProbe can kill a container during initialization before it is ready. Without readinessProbe, load balancers send traffic to pods that are not yet serving. Without livenessProbe, deadlocked processes accumulate.

example.code: startupProbe: httpGet: path: /healthz port: 8080 failureThreshold: 30 # up to 5 minutes to start periodSeconds: 10 readinessProbe: httpGet: path: /ready port: 8080 initialDelaySeconds: 5 periodSeconds: 5 failureThreshold: 3 livenessProbe: httpGet: path: /healthz port: 8080 initialDelaySeconds: 30 periodSeconds: 15

rule.statement: Every container must have all four security context fields set: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities.drop: ["ALL"].
rule.rationale: These four together prevent the most common container escape paths. runAsNonRoot prevents trivial UID 0 exploitation. readOnlyRootFilesystem prevents persistence. drop ALL capabilities removes Linux kernel attack surface. allowPrivilegeEscalation: false prevents setuid escalation.

example.code: securityContext: runAsNonRoot: true runAsUser: 1000 readOnlyRootFilesystem: true allowPrivilegeEscalation: false capabilities: drop: ["ALL"] seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault

section.title: High Availability and Disruption

rule.statement: Define a PodDisruptionBudget for every Deployment with more than one replica; set minAvailable to at least 1 (or maxUnavailable to 1 for larger deployments).
rule.rationale: Without a PDB, a node drain during maintenance can remove all replicas simultaneously, causing a complete outage. PDB ensures at least one replica stays healthy during voluntary disruptions.

rule.statement: Never use latest as image tag in production; pin to a specific immutable tag (digest or version).
rule.rationale: latest is mutable — the image it points to can change between pod restarts. A node restart can pull a different image version than the currently running pods, causing split-brain versions in a Deployment.

section.reference: Production Deployment with full security context, probes, PDB
